step.title: Step 32
step.actionDisplay: MOUSE(row=44, col=21) -> MOUSE(row=44, col=31) -> MOUSE(row=44, col=41) -> RESET

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=44, col=21), MOUSE(row=44, col=31), MOUSE(row=44, col=41).
You are still on the same level.
The game is over.
Current state: step 63, level 1.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 62 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 1.9x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 62 actions returned the board to a state already seen 62 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 62 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
GAME_OVER! The game is over. This is a crucial finding.
The game over was triggered by clicking button 5 (col 41) as the 3rd action in the sequence 1, 3, 5.
Looking at the timer bar at the top, it's now fully blue (was partially blue before). This suggests the timer bar represents remaining moves or time, and it's now full, meaning I've used up all my moves.
Wait, but the timer bar was blue on the left and gray on the right before. Now it's fully blue. This suggests the timer bar fills up as I make moves, and when it's full, the game is over.
So the game has a limited number of moves, and I've used them all up without completing the level.
But wait, the game is over, not the level completed. This means I failed the level.
Let me think about what this means. The game has a limited number of moves (perhaps 64, corresponding to the 64x64 grid or some other limit). I've used 62 actions, and the game is over.
But the level wasn't completed. This means I need to restart the level and try a different approach.
Actually, looking at the image, the board looks the same as before. The game is over, but the board hasn't changed. This suggests the game over state is just a flag, and I can still interact with the board.
But the result says `game_over: True` and `done: False`. This is confusing. Let me re-examine.
Actually, `done: False` means the game session is not fully done. `game_over: True` means the current level/game state is over. But I can still take actions.
Let me try clicking a button to see if the game resets or if I can continue playing.
Actually, wait. The timer bar is now fully blue. This might mean the game has reset or the timer has been exhausted. Let me try clicking a button to see what happens.
But first, let me think about what I've learned:
1. The game has a limited number of moves (the timer bar fills up as I make moves).
2. When the timer bar is full, the game is over.
3. I've used 62 actions without completing the level.
4. The level must be completable in fewer than 62 actions (probably around 32 or fewer, based on the efficiency budget).
So the key insight is that I need to find the correct button pattern in fewer moves. The timer bar limits the number of moves I can make.
Now, what is the correct button pattern? I've tried many patterns without success. Let me think about what I haven't tried.
Actually, I just realized something. The game over was triggered by the 3rd action in the sequence 1, 3, 5. But the previous actions also contributed to filling the timer bar. So the game over is not specifically triggered by clicking button 5, but by exhausting the timer bar.
Let me re-examine the timer bar. It was partially blue before, and now it's fully blue. This suggests the timer bar fills up linearly with each action.
If the timer bar has 64 segments (corresponding to the 64x64 grid), and I've used 62 actions, then the timer bar would be almost full. But the image shows the timer bar is fully blue, which suggests the timer bar is now at 100%.
Wait, but the timer bar was blue on the left and gray on the right before. Now it's fully blue. This suggests the timer bar fills from left to right, and when it's full, the game is over.
So the game has a limited number of moves, and I've used them all up.
Now, the question is: how do I restart the level? Or is the game over permanent?
Let me try clicking a button to see if the game resets or if I can continue playing.
Actually, I think the game over is permanent for this level. I need to restart the entire game to try again.
But wait, the result says `done: False`. This might mean the game session is not fully done, and I can continue to the next level or restart the current level.
Let me try clicking a button to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game is over. Let me check if I can still interact with the board.
# Try clicking button 1 to see what happens.
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 44, 'col': 21}])

print("Result:", last_action_result)
print("Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
print("Game over:", last_action_result.get('game_over'))
print("Done:", last_action_result.get('done'))
print("Board changed:", last_action_result.get('board_changed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
